coredata.dc:title: Amorphous Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres for highly sensitive non-enzymatic glucose detection prepared via CQDs induced aggregation process
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Jiajie
Yin, Haoyong
Cui, Zhenzhen
Qin, Dongyu
Gong, Jianying
Nie, Qiulin
coredata.dc:description: Non-enzymatic electrochemical sensors for the detection of glucose were designed based on amorphous Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres. The amorphous Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres were prepared by a CQDs assistant crystallization inhibition process. The morphologies and composition of the microspheres were characterized by SEM, TEM, XRD, EDS, and TG/DSC. The results showed that the microspheres had uniform heterogeneous phases with amorphous Ni(OH)2 and CQDs. The sensor based on amorphous Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres showed remarkable electrocatalytic activity towards glucose oxidation comparing to the conventional crystalline Ni(OH)2, which included two linear range (20μM–350μM and 0.45mM–2.5mM) with high selectivity of 2760.05 and 1853.64μAmM−1cm−2. Moreover, the interference from the commonly interfering species such as urea, ascorbic acid, NaCl, L-proline and L-Valine, can be effectively avoided. The high sensitivity, wide glucose detection range and good selectivity of the electrode may be due to their synergistic effect of amorphous phase and CQDs incorporation. These findings may promote the application of amorphous Ni(OH)2 as advanced electrochemical glucose sensing materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Amorphous Ni(OH)2
Carbon quantum dots
Nonenzymatic
Glucose sensor

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The illustration of formation mechanisms of Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres and Ni(OH)2 microflowers.

SEM images of Ni(OH)2 microflowers (a), (b) and Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres (c), (d). Inserts (b) and (d) are the optical photographs of Ni(OH)2 microflowers and Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres. Insert (c) is the optical image of CQDs solution.

TEM (a) and HRTEM (b) images of Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres. (c)STEM image and its corresponding C, O, Ni element mappings for Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres. (d) The SAED pattern of Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres.

(a) Cyclic voltammogram of Ni(OH)2 microflowers and Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres in presence of glucose in 0.1M NaOH. Insert is the enlarged cyclic voltammogram of Ni(OH)2 microflowers. (b) Cyclic voltammogram of Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres with different concentration of glucose. (c) Schematic illustration of glucose electrocatalytic reaction mechanism on Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres electrode.
Cyclic voltammogram of Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres at different scan rates (a) and the plots of peak currents as a function of the scan rate (b).
Amperometric responses of Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres with stepwise addition of glucose into 0.1M NaOH solution (a) and calibration curve for current vs. concentration of glucose (b).

Amperometric responses of Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres on addition of 0.2mM glucose, 0.04mM urea, 0.04mM ascorbic acid, 0.3mM NaCl, 0.3mM L-proline, 0.3mM L-valine and 0.2mM glucose respectively into 0.1M NaOH solution.
Nyquist (a) and bode (b) plots of Ni(OH)2 and Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres in 0.1M NaOH solution. Insets in (a) are the accordingly equivalent circuits of Ni(OH)2 (I) and Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres (II).

head.ce:title: Amorphous Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres for highly sensitive non-enzymatic glucose detection prepared via CQDs induced aggregation process

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials & Environmental Engineering, Hangzhou Dianzi University, Hangzhou, 310018, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of physics, Hangzhou Dianzi University, Hangzhou, 310018, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Non-enzymatic electrochemical sensors for the detection of glucose were designed based on amorphous Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres. The amorphous Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres were prepared by a CQDs assistant crystallization inhibition process. The morphologies and composition of the microspheres were characterized by SEM, TEM, XRD, EDS, and TG/DSC. The results showed that the microspheres had uniform heterogeneous phases with amorphous Ni(OH)2 and CQDs. The sensor based on amorphous Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres showed remarkable electrocatalytic activity towards glucose oxidation comparing to the conventional crystalline Ni(OH)2, which included two linear range (20μM–350μM and 0.45mM–2.5mM) with high selectivity of 2760.05 and 1853.64μAmM−1cm−2. Moreover, the interference from the commonly interfering species such as urea, ascorbic acid, NaCl, L-proline and L-Valine, can be effectively avoided. The high sensitivity, wide glucose detection range and good selectivity of the electrode may be due to their synergistic effect of amorphous phase and CQDs incorporation. These findings may promote the application of amorphous Ni(OH)2 as advanced electrochemical glucose sensing materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Diabetes mellitus, as one of the leading causes of death and disability, has led to more and more attention of accurate, fast, and reliable measurement of glucose, because it can be largely controlled and managed by closely monitoring the blood glucose concentration [1]. Great efforts have been contributed to the development of electrocatalytic glucose sensor, which is a powerful, simple, and low-cost technique among other methods. Although the enzyme-based glucose sensors are more popular in commercial application due to their high selectivity and excellent sensitivity [2,3], some intrinsic drawbacks, such as the difficulty of immobilizing enzyme, chemical instability and expensive enzymes, still limit their further applications [4]. Alternatively, non-enzymatic electrochemical sensors, based on directly electrocatalytic oxidation of glucose without enzyme, have been extensively studied and show considerable stability and simplicity [5,6].

Among various active electrode materials, Ni(OH)2 has been widely investigated due to its remarkable activity for glucose sensing, for it can easily form the redox couple of Ni(OH)2/NiOOH and then the glucose can be easily oxidized by NiOOH [7]. Up to now, almost all of the investigations are concentrated on crystalline Ni(OH)2 materials, mainly due to their flexibility in forming complex three-dimensional (3D) structures and their excellent electrochemical properties in glucose sensing [8–10]. Moreover, amorphous phase Ni(OH)2 is usually evaluated to be not suitable for electrocatalytic glucose sensing for its poor electrochemical performance. However, the amorphous phase Ni(OH)2 was recently demostrated to have impressive electrocatalytic performance, which may be used in certain device applications [11–15]. For instance, amorphous Ni(OH)2 nanospheres [11] and amorphous Ni(OH)2@three-dimensional Ni core-shell structures [14] were both reported to have ultrahigh capacitance and energy density as electrochemical pseudocapacitor materials which may be due to the disorder in their amorphous phase. Amorphous Ni(OH)2 nanosheets also showed ultrahigh activity and super-long-term cycle stability as advanced water oxidation catalysts [13]. Interestingly, an improved electrochemical sensing ability for glucose sensing was also investigated on the amorphous Ni(OH)2 nanoboxes, which might be due to their amorphous and hollow structural features [15]. Therefore, it is very promising to develop non-enzymatic electrochemical glucose sensors based on amorphous Ni(OH)2 electrode materials with different morphology and structures.

Although the amorphous Ni(OH)2 demonstrated desirable electrocatalytic performance in glucose sensing [15], the preparation of amorphous Ni(OH)2 always needs high-tech synthetic methods such as electrodeposition [11,13,14], templates sacrifice [15,16] or micro-emulsion precipitation method [17], which are either expensive or difficult to scale-up for large production volumes. It is well known that the containing particles dispersed in some matrix usually represent the most frequent form of the stabilization of an amorphous metal oxide or hydroxide phase [18]. Moreover, the narrow particle size distribution and suppressed interparticle interactions are the main advantages of such nanocomposites in comparison with the powders. Particularly, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), with highly water solubility and stability due to the coated hydroxyl and carboxyl [19], may be a better candidate as the amorphous stabilizer to form amorphous Ni(OH)2/CQDs nano- or micro- composites. In addition, the outstanding electronic, and electrochemical properties of CQDs [20] may also introduce enhanced electrochemical glucose sensing performance to amorphous Ni(OH)2/CQDs composites. Combination of CQDs with octahedral Cu2O has already been proved showing not only the enhanced electrocatalytic behavior but also enhanced sensitivity and stability in glucose and H2O2 sensing due to the synergistic effect of CQDs and Cu2O [21]. To the best of our knowledge, there have no reports on the preparation of amorphous Ni(OH)2/CQDs composites for non-enzymatic glucose sensing. Hence, it is highly desired to combine CQDs with amorphous Ni(OH)2 to improve the performance of conventional Ni(OH)2 non-enzymatic glucose sensing electrodes.

In this paper, we demonstrated a simple and effective approach to synthesize amorphous Ni(OH)2/CQDs composites microspheres with CQDs acting as amorphous matrix stabilizers. The amorphous Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres showed excellent electrochemical performance in non-enzymatic glucose sensing. The highly enhanced glucose sensing performance against crystal Ni(OH)2 microflowers may be due to the synergistic effect of CQDs and amorphous Ni(OH)2. The design idea of this kind of amorphous Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres may be beneficial to the preparation of other high performance non-enzymatic glucose sensing materials.

The preparation of CQDs was carried out using glucose as carbon source. Typically, 40mL of water containing 3g of glucose was firstly transferred into a 50mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. Then the autoclave was treated in the oven at 160°C for 0.5h and subsequently cooled down to room temperature naturally. Finally, the solution was centrifuged to remove the precipitants and then the supernatant was filtered through a polytetrafluoroethylene membrane and the filtrate was dialyzed in 1000Da dialysis bag for 5days. The purified solution was marked as CQDs solution and was further used to prepare amorphous Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres.

The as prepared CQDs solution(25mL) was firstly diluted by 55mL deionized water and then NiCl2·6H2O (0.0952g) were dispersed in the solution with continuously ultrasonication for 0.5h. The solution was further stirred for another 0.5h to ensure the sufficient adsorption of Ni2+ on the CQDs. Then urea (0.5g) was added into the solution under stirring with continuously stirring for 0.5h. After that, the solution was transferred into a Teflon autoclave and kept at 90°C for 8h. Finally, the suspension was cooled naturally to room temperature and products were collected and washed with deionized water and ethanol several times and dried in air at 80°C to obtain the amorphous Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres. For comparison the Ni(OH)2 microflowers were prepared in the same procedure without the addition of CQDs.

The morphologies and microstructures of the obtained products were characterized by field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM, a Hitachi S-4700) with scanning voltages of 15kV and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEOL 200CX) with accelerating voltage of 200kV. The crystal phase of the samples was identified by X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Thermo ARL SCINTAG X'TRA) with CuKα radiation (λ=0.154056nm) under 40kV. Differential scanning calorimetry/thermogravimetry (TG/DSC1, METTLER) analysis was conducted at a heating rate of 10K/min with a flowing N2 gas stream at 100mL/min. Electrochemical measurements were performed with a CHI630D analyzer (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co. China) and Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analysis were measured on an electrochemical workstation (Zennium, Zahner,Germany).

All the electrochemical experiments were performed using a three-electrode electrochemical system: the modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE), an Ag/AgCl (saturated KCl) and a platinum plate (0.5×0.5cm) as working, reference and counter electrode, respectively. NaOH (0.1M) was used as electrolyte being deoxygenated with highly pure N2 for at least 15min before measurements. Cyclic voltammograms (CVs) were recorded between 0.3 and 0.7V at different scanning rate of 20–90mV/s. Chronoamperometry (CA) were executed at an applied potential of 0.6V with the scanning rate of 50mV/s. The measurement was performed with N2 continuously bubbling in the solution which can both remove the dissolved oxygen and simultaneously act as stirring process.

The working electrodes were prepared firstly by polishing GCE (3mm diameter) with 0.3- and 0.05-μm alumina slurry and rinsing thoroughly with deionized water. Then the GCE was sonicated in 1:1 nitric acid, acetone and deionized water respectively, and dried at room temperature. Finally, 20μL of the amorphous Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres solution (5mg catalyst, 20μL Nafion and 5mL ethanol) was dropped onto the cleaned GCE and firstly dried at room temperature and then at 50°C in oven.

Fig. 1 shows the SEM images of Ni(OH)2 prepared with and without the addition of CQDs. It can be seen that the morphology of Ni(OH)2 without CQDs was flower-shaped structure with the diameter ranging from 1.5μm to 2μm. Enlarged SEM image of Ni(OH)2 microflowers reveals that it was composed by a lot of nanoplates with the thickness less than 50nm. However, the morphology of Ni(OH)2 with CQDs is quite different from the Ni(OH)2 microflowers. As shown in Fig. 1c, uniform and smooth spheres with diameters ranging from 1μm to 1.5μm can be investigated. The enlarged SEM image in Fig. 1d clearly shows the Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres have homogeneous surface. EDS analysis was carried out to investigate the elemental composition of Ni(OH)2 microflowers and Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres. Fig. S1 exhibits the presence of C, O and Ni elements on both Ni(OH)2 microflowers and Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres EDX patterns. Although the C peak may come from organic adsorption on the sample or substrate, the abnormally high ratio of C on the Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres may indicate the combination of CQDs with Ni(OH)2. The inserts in Fig. 1c and d are the photo images of the Ni(OH)2 microflowers and Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres whose colors may further indicate the formation of pure Ni(OH)2 microflowers (pale green) and composites Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres (black). The optical photograph of CQDs solution was also inserted in Fig. 1c. The clear and transparent yellow solution may suggest the homogeneous CQDs were formed in the solution.

The detailed crystal structures of the as prepared Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres were further examined using TEM (Fig. 2 ). It can be seen that highly uniform and smooth microspheres with diameters of 1–1.5μm can also be observed from the images, which is in agreement with the SEM findings in Fig. 1c. The detailed structure of the Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres (Fig. 2b) reveal the microspheres have inhomogeneous surfaces. However, the highly magnified HRTEM (Insert in Fig. 2b) shows there was no crystalline morphology detected in the bright-field TEM image of the sample, which may suggest the amorphous phase of the microspheres. In a corresponding selected-area electron-diffraction pattern (Fig. 2d), a broad and diffused halo ring can be observed which may further suggest an amorphous morphology. Moreover, scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) and elemental mapping analysis were also performed to analyze the morphology and composition of the Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres. The STEM images of Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres and corresponding EDS mapping of C, O and Ni elements indicated that the three ions are accompanied by and uniformly distributed in the microspheres which may suggest the well-distribution of Ni(OH)2 and CQDs in the composite microspheres.

The XRD patterns for Ni(OH)2 microflowers and Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres were presented in Fig. S2. The relative high intensity of the corresponding diffraction peaks for Ni(OH)2 microflowers suggests the high crystallinity and high purity of the as prepared Ni(OH)2. Nevertheless, the corresponding XRD pattern of Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres shows that there are no characteristic peaks of crystalline Ni(OH)2, which suggests that the CQDs induced Ni(OH)2 is amorphous in nature as expected. Fig. S3 shows the TG/DSC curves of Ni(OH)2 microflowers and amorphous Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres, which can be seen that there are two weight loss regions on TG curve of Ni(OH)2 microflowers (Fig. S3a). The first region under 200°C may be attributed to the remove of the structurally bonded H2O. The relatively higher weight loss and lager endothermic peak of amorphous Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres might be due to the amorphous phase of Ni(OH)2 and combination of CQDs, which may induce more absorbed H2O or organic molecular and the probable phase change of the amorphous Ni(OH)2. The second region between 250°C and 400°C is mainly due to the decomposition of Ni(OH)2 to NiO. The NiO calcination process may occur above 400°C, which almost no weight loss observed on the curve of Ni(OH)2 microflowers. However, similar with the first two regions weight loss of Ni(OH)2 microflowers there is also an obvious weight loss above 400°C for amorphous Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres, which may be due to the remove of combined CQDs. The relatively high total weight loss of amorphous Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres to Ni(OH)2 microflowers (68.5% to 31.3%) may provide another indirect evidence of combination of amorphous Ni(OH)2 and CQDs this composite microspheres.

The possible formation mechanism of amorphous Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres may be schematically illustrated in Scheme 1 . Without the addition of CQDs the general process for the fabricating crystalline Ni(OH)2 microflowers could undergo the following steps: the formation of amorphous Ni(OH)2 nanoparticles, random aggregation and anisotropic crystal growth to microflowers during the hydrothermal treatment. However, when CQDs were in advance introduced the solution, the Ni2+ may firstly adsorbed on the surface of CQDs and then the composite CQDs/amorphous Ni(OH)2 nanoparticles can be formed in the solution which may also subsequently undergo the random aggregation process. However, during the hydrothermal treatment the anisotropic crystal growth of CQDs/amorphous Ni(OH)2 nanoparticles could be greatly inhibited due to the largely heterogeneous constituent of the materials, which may undergo the further amorphous aggregation process, therefor the amorphous Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres can be formed in this CQDs assistant method.

The electrochemical performance of amorphous Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres towards glucose in an alkaline medium was examined and compared with the Ni(OH)2 microflowers. Fig. 3 a shows the current responses of amorphous Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres and Ni(OH)2 microflowers towards 1.0mM of glucose in 0.1M NaOH. It can be seen that the catalytic current of amorphous Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres towards glucose oxidation is much higher than that of Ni(OH)2 microflowers, which indicated that the amorphous Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres exhibited strong electrocatalytic activity to glucose. The enhancement of electro-oxidation of glucose at amorphous Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres may be attributed to synergistic effect of both amorphous Ni(OH)2 and CQDs.

The catalytic property of amorphous Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres was further investigated at various concentrations of glucose in 0.1M NaOH. As shown in Fig. 3b, the oxidation peak currents increased with increasing concentrations of glucose, which indicates that the amorphous Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres may be used as non-enzymatic glucose sensing materials. It is noticeable that the peak potential also shifted more positively which may be due to the more energy-demanding diffusion of glucose at the modified electrode surface [22,23]. As is known that the Ni(OH)2/NiOOH redox couple in an alkaline medium was responsible for the oxidation of glucose to glucolactone [23]. Glucose sensing mechanism at Ni(OH)2/CQDs/GCE can be schematically illustrated in Fig. 3c. During CV scan Ni(OH)2 can firstly lost an electron to become NiO(OH) by the fast electron transferring through Ni(OH)2/CQDs. Subsequently, when glucose diffuses to the electrode surface, it will be rapidly oxidized to glucolactone by the NiO(OH) on the electrode. Therefore, the specific equation of glucose oxidation on the electrode may be represented as the followings [24]:
(1) Ni(OH)2 +OH− →NiOOH+H2O+e−
(2) NiOOH+glucose→Ni(OH)2 +glucolactone

The influence of scan rate on the current response at the amorphous Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres was also investigated. Fig. 4 a shows the voltammetric behavior of the amorphous Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres electrode in glucose at scan rates from 20mV/s to 90mV/s. It can be observed on Fig. 4a that as the increase of scan rate both the anodic and the cathodic peak currents changed proportionally with the corresponding anodic and cathodic peak potentials shifting toward the positive and negative sides, respectively, thus leading to a larger peak-to-peak separation. Further investigation (Fig. 4b) indicates that the oxidative and reductive peak current are proportional to the square root of the scan rate, which implied that the electrocatalytic reaction is under diffusional control of glucose and related to the glucose concentration in the bulk solution[25].

Under optimized conditions, chronoamperometric measurements were used to determine the dynamic detection range and sensitivity of amorphous Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres toward electrocatalysis of glucose. Fig. 5 a shows a typical current–time plot of the amorphous Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres electrode on successive additions of glucose. It can be found that there is an obvious oxidation currents increase with continuously increase in the concentration of glucose. Moreover, the amorphous Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres electrode exhibits greatly higher glucose sensitivity than that of Ni(OH)2 microflowers electrode, which is also consistent with the cyclic voltammetry results. After addition of glucose, oxidation current immediately increased and reached 95% of steady-state current with in 6s, suggesting the sensitive and rapid response of amorphous Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres electrode to glucose oxidation reaction. Fig. 5b displays the calibration curve of glucose at the amorphous Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres electrode. It can be seen that the oxidation peak current of glucose increased with its concentration in the range from 20μM to 2500μM with two linear ranges. The first was at low glucose levels (20μM–350μM) with a sensitive of 2760.05μAmM−1cm−2 and a correlation of 0.999. The linear regression equation obtained on amorphous Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres electrode is Ipa/μA cm−2 =0.04077/μA cm−2 +2760.04708 [glucose]/μA mM−1cm−2. The second was observed at higher glucose concentrations (0.45mM-2.5mM) with a sensitive of 1853.64μAmM−1cm−2 and a correlation of 0.998. The linear regression equation is Ipa/μA cm−2 =0.55335/μA cm−2 +1853.63818 [glucose]/μA mM−1cm−2. From the fitting results, the sensitivities of both ranges are about 2760.05μAmM−1cm−2 and 1853.64μAmM−1cm−2 which indicates the amorphous Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres electrode has a much higher sensitivity and a wide range of glucose detection.

Selectivity of detection is considered equally important for a sensor to be applied in real sample monitoring. The interference test from electroactive compounds normally coexisting with glucose in real sample such as urea, ascorbic acid, NaCl, L-proline and L-Valine, was carried out as shown in Fig. 6 . It can be found that the amperometric response of the amorphous Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres electrode to glucose was not affected by addition of urea, ascorbic acid, L-proline and L-Valine. It was reported that NaCl can cause the inactivation of some metal or alloy electrode in the detection of glucose [26]. However, the results indicated that these foreign substances including NaCl in this experimental condition did not interfere significantly on the detection of glucose.

The interfacial properties of the GCE electrodes modified with amorphous Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres and Ni(OH)2 microflowers were studied by EIS. Fig. 7 shows the Nyquist and Bode plots of amorphous Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres and Ni(OH)2 microflowers in 0.1M NaOH supporting electrolyte containing 1mM glucose in the frequency range from 105 to 0.01Hz. The AC voltage amplitude was 5mV and the applied potential was 0.55V. The proposed equivalent circuits of Ni(OH)2 microflowers (I) and Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres (II) were also displayed in the insert of Fig. 7a. Rs was the solution resistance, Rc was the coating resistance and Rct represented the charge transfer resistances. The constant phase elements (CPE) modelled the nonideal double-layer capacitances in which CPEe was the constant phase element of the electrode and CPEc represented the constant phase element of coating. The impedance of a Faradaic reaction in this circuits consists of an active charge transfer resistance Rct and a specific electrochemical element of diffusion Rw (Warburg element), which is similar with the CQDs modified Cu2O electrodes [21]. It can be concluded from the EIS curve and the fitting with the equivalent circuits that the electron transfer resistance of Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres electrode is much less than the Ni(OH)2 microflowers. Therefore, the Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres electrode should have higher electrochemical activity than the Ni(OH)2 microflowers electrode, which may also the reason of enhancing the electrochemical glucose sensing performance of amorphous Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres.

The amorphous Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres were prepared through the CQDs assistant hydrothermal process. The amorphous Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres with diameter of 1-1.5μm have an inhomogeneous surfaces accompanied by sparsely distributed amorphous Ni(OH)2 nanoparticles and CQDs. The amorphous Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres modified electrode revealed remarkable performance towards non-enzymatic glucose detection in an alkaline solution, which may be due to its higher electronic conductivity arriving from the synergistic effect of amorphous Ni(OH)2 and CQDs. The electrode conveyed excellent sensing performance of non-enzymatic glucose detection which included two linear range (20μM–350μM and 0.45mM-2.5mM) with high selectivity of 2760.05 and 1853.64μAmM−1cm−2. The sensor also showed highly selective to glucose in the presence of commonly interfering species such as urea, ascorbic acid, NaCl, L-proline and L-Valine, which present the amorphous Ni(OH)2/CQDs microspheres as one of the promising microstructures for electrochemical biosensor devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by Educational Commission of zhejiang Province (No.Y201329269) and National Nature Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province (No. LY12B07001).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2017.05.129.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical-enzymatic analysis of blood glucose and lactate
Electron-transfer mediator for a NAD-glucose dehydrogenase-based glucose sensor
Non-enzymatic glucose detection free of ascorbic acid interference using nickel powder and nafion sol-gel dispersed renewable carbon ceramic electrode
Graphene wrapped Cu2O nanocubes: non-enzymatic electrochemical sensors for the detection of glucose and hydrogen peroxide with enhanced stability
MWCNT-ruthenium oxide composite paste electrode as non-enzymatic glucose sensor
Fast conversion of redox couple on Ni(OH)2/C nanocomposite electrode for high-performance nonenzymatic glucose sensor
Preparation of reduced graphene oxide-Ni(OH)2 composites by electrophoretic deposition: application for non-enzymatic glucose sensing
A nonenzymatic electrochemical glucose sensor based on Ni(OH)2-CNT-PVDF composite and its application in measuring serum glucose
Nickel hydroxide nanoflowers for a nonenzymatic electrochemical glucose sensor
Amorphous nickel hydroxide nanospheres with ultrahigh capacitance and energy density as electrochemical pseudocapacitor materials
Facile synthesis of amorphous Ni(OH)2 for high-performance supercapacitors via electrochemical assembly in a reverse micelle
Amorphous nickel hydroxide nanosheets with ultrahigh activity and super-long-term cycle stability as advanced water oxidation catalysts
Amorphous Ni(OH)2@ three-dimensional Ni core-shell nanostructures for high capacitance pseudocapacitors and asymmetric supercapacitors
Amorphous Ni(OH)2 nanoboxes: fast fabrication and enhanced sensing for glucose
High-performance supercapacitor electrode based on amorphous mesoporous Ni(OH)2 nanoboxes
Synthesis and characterization of amorphous alpha-nickel hydroxide
Water-soluble fluorescent carbon quantum dots and photocatalyst design
Carbon nanodots: synthesis, properties and applications
Carbon quantum dots/octahedral Cu2O nanocomposites for non-enzymatic glucose and hydrogen peroxide amperometric sensor
Ni foam: a novel three-dimensional porous sensing platform for sensitive and selective nonenzymatic glucose detection
Highly sensitive non-enzymatic glucose sensor based on over-oxidized polypyrrole nanowires modified with Ni(OH)2 nanoflakes
Synthesis of hierarchical Ni(OH)2 hollow nanorod via chemical bath deposition and its glucose sensing performance
Three-dimensional roselike alpha-Ni(OH)2 assembled from nanosheet building blocks for non-enzymatic glucose detection
Combinatorial discovery of alloy electrocatalysts for amperometric glucose sensors
